content.problemStatement:
You are given two integers NNN and KKK.

Construct an array AAA consisting of NNN **distinct** positive integers such that the following conditions are met:


1≤Ai≤2⋅104;1 \leq A_i \leq 2 \cdot 10^4;1≤Ai​≤2⋅104;
There exists **no** subsequence of AAA with sum KKK.
It can be shown that the answer always exists.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT: the number of test cases.
The only line of each test case contains two integers NNN and KKK - as mentioned in the statement.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing NNN space-separated elements: A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. If there are multiple answers, you may print any.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two integers N and K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with N distinct integers A1 … AN (1 ≤ Ai ≤ 20000) such that no subsequence of the array has sum exactly K.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first case we add 1,2,3 (sum=6<10) then need one more element, we add 20000 (>K). No subset sums to 10. For the second case the sum of 1..10 is 55<1000000, so we can output the first ten natural numbers.

solution.approach:
We build the array in two phases. 1) Add the smallest unused numbers starting from 1 while the total sum of the chosen numbers stays strictly less than K. After this phase the sum of any subset consisting only of these numbers is also < K, therefore K cannot be formed using them. 2) If we still need more elements, we add large numbers starting from 20000 downwards (skipping K if it lies in this range). Every such large number is > K, so any subset containing at least one of them has sum > K. Consequently K cannot be obtained from any subset of the final array. All numbers are distinct and respect the bound 1 ≤ Ai ≤ 20000.
The algorithm is deterministic, runs in O(N) per test case and uses O(N) extra space for the answer.
